checks.a_analysis: I have compared the output JSON with the source PDF page by page. All text present in the JSON elements, including the headings and table content, has a direct correspondence in the source document. The repetition of the document title at the start of the second section reflects its presence at the top of the second page in the source PDF. No fabricated text was found.

The source document contains no contact information such as phone numbers, email addresses, or physical addresses. Consequently, the JSON output correctly contains no such information. No fabrications were detected.

The source PDF consists of two pages containing a continuous table split across the pages. The JSON output includes two main sections corresponding to these two pages. All framework layers and 'other important layers' listed in the source are present in the JSON. No sections or pages are missing.

The source PDF contains a table split over two pages. Page 1 contains 29 distinct rows (including headers and subheaders). Page 2 contains 11 rows (the continuation of the 'Wetlands' description plus 10 new layer rows). The JSON output represents this as two tables with 29 rows and 11 rows respectively, matching the source row count exactly.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I verified the text content of each row against the source. Even complex descriptions like those for ETJs, Soils, and NPDES are captured in full without omission. The split description for 'Wetlands' across the page break is captured across the two tables in the JSON, preserving all text.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content in the JSON is a high-fidelity representation of the source. Punctuation, capitalization, and technical acronyms (e.g., SSURGO, NPDES, TINs) are preserved correctly. No changes that alter the meaning of the information were identified.

checks.a_analysis: The title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' appears at the top of both pages in the source document. The output correctly reflects this intentional repetition by including the heading at the start of each section. No other content duplication was observed.
The output uses H1 for the main titles on both pages. This is a consistent and logical flat hierarchy for a two-page document where each page begins with the title. No heading levels are skipped.

The table structure is corrupted at the transition between page 1 and page 2. In the source, the row for 'Wetlands' starts on page 1 and its description concludes on page 2. In the output, the continuation text 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' is placed in a new table at the start of section 2 as a single-column header row. This breaks the 2-column grid of the data and misidentifies a cell's data as a table header.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table continues on Page 2 with 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' in the right-hand column of the first row.
checks.b_evidence_output: In section 2, the table (reading_order 5) starts with a single cell header row: { "cell_type": "header", "content": [ { "text": "Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory"... } ], "colspan": 1 }
checks.b_evidence_location: Page 2, first row of the table / Section 2, element index 1

checks.a_analysis: The output follows the logical flow of the source document, moving from the title into the table rows in the order they appear on the page.
There are no formal bulleted or numbered lists in the source document; the content is entirely contained within tables. The output correctly identifies this structure.
The source document contains no hyperlinks or URLs. The output correctly reflects this by not including any link elements.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output breaks the visual flow of information present in the source PDF. The entry for 'Wetlands' is split between two tables across two separate sections. The second half of the description for 'Wetlands' ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'), which appears at the top of page 2 in the PDF, is used as a header for a new table in the HTML. This disrupts the logical and visual organization of the list.
checks.b_evidence_source: The PDF shows 'Wetlands' on page 1 with its description continuing onto page 2: 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and' (p1) 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' (p2).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML first table ends with 'Wetlands' | 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and'. A new section then repeats the document title and begins a second table where 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' is the header.
checks.b_evidence_location: Transition between the end of the first table and the start of the second section/table.

checks.a_analysis: The second table in the HTML output is visually and structurally broken. It uses text that was intended to be the continuation of a data cell from the previous page ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory') as its column header. This results in a table where the first column header is incorrect content and the data rows underneath are misaligned with the header's implied meaning.
checks.b_evidence_source: In the PDF, 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' is plain text at the top of page 2, continuing the 'Wetlands' row.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML, the second table has a header row containing 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'.
checks.b_evidence_location: The header row of the second table in the HTML output.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF contains no meaningful images, charts, or logos; it is entirely composed of text and tables. The HTML output correctly reflects this absence.

The document title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' is repeated in the HTML. However, per the instructions, document titles appearing as section headings (to denote the start of content from a new PDF page) is expected accessible design and not considered a duplication defect. No actual body content (paragraphs or rows) is duplicated.
